findings.finding: The plan introduces a new blocking gate on the open->in_progress (claim) path for an existing, in-use rebar feature, but never specifies how this behavior change is rolled out to clients. There is no flag/canary/staged-rollout mechanism described for turning the gate ON; the AC reads as an absolute 'claiming a ticket requires a valid signed attestation,' implying a single flip from 'no gate' to 'gate blocks all claims.'
evidence: Success criterion: 'Claiming a ticket (open->in_progress) requires a valid, non-stale signed plan-review attestation; absence ... blocks the claim'
AC: 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check with --force+justification override'
The plan never states a staged-rollout / feature-flag / canary mechanism for ENABLING the gate itself across the existing client base
scenarios: The gate ships enabled-by-default and every existing in-flight ticket (claimed before the gate existed, with no attestation) suddenly blocks on claim across the entire diverse client base
A client upgrades rebar and all their open tickets become unclaimable without a --force override they didn't know they needed
findings.impact: A one-shot behavior change to the claim path for all users with no staged enablement could break existing workflows org-wide on upgrade, the exact MAJOR pattern T12(a) targets.

findings.finding: Rollback/reversibility of the gate as a runtime change is never specified. The plan describes per-criterion config and --force per-claim, but offers no documented cheap, tested global kill-switch to disable the gate quickly if it misfires in production. The --force override is per-ticket-per-claim and requires justification, which is not a cheap global undo.
evidence: The plan never states a 'disable the gate' config flag or kill switch; config knobs listed are 'risk posture, chunking, budget caps, and per-criterion blocking'
--force is described as a per-claim escape ('a recorded --force override available'), not a global disable
Follow-on F2 'auto-demote blocking criteria on FP ceiling' is deferred OUT of this epic, leaving no in-epic remediation lever
scenarios: The DET Layer-1 floor produces an unexpected false-positive block pattern in production and operators need to turn the gate off fleet-wide immediately
A client hits cost/latency problems and wants to revert to pre-gate behavior without rolling back the whole rebar version
findings.impact: Without an explicit, cheap, tested rollback path, a misbehaving gate has no fast undo short of a full version rollback, violating T12(b).

findings.finding: The attestation/signature introduces a stateful artifact (signed events, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar events, material-commit binding) but the plan does not specify deploy ordering or old+new coexistence: how tickets created/claimed before the gate (no attestation events) coexist with the new claim-check after rollout, and how reviewers/CI running mixed rebar versions interpret signatures.
evidence: Attestation is 'Bound to the latest MATERIAL event commit' and a 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-IGNORED SIDECAR event' is added
AC mentions 'reducer skips the type; head/.cache/deps/validate unaffected' but does not address pre-existing tickets that have no attestation event during the transition
The plan never states a migration/coexistence story for tickets in flight at gate-enablement time, nor for clients on older rebar versions encountering the new event type / signature
scenarios: A CI process on an old rebar version encounters the new REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event type or signed attestation it doesn't understand
Tickets claimed and in_progress before the gate must be re-reviewed retroactively, or are grandfathered — the plan doesn't say which
findings.impact: Unspecified deploy ordering / old+new coexistence can cause reducer or CI errors and inconsistent enforcement during the rollout window, the gap T12(c) targets.
